annotations.label: Product limit definition

anchor.snippet: Product limit. Define

annotations.note: Mathlib defines Real.exp / NormedSpace.exp via a power series, not as the product limit (1 + x/n)^n; no declaration formalizes this alternative definition.

annotations.label: Power series definition

anchor.snippet: Power series . Define e x as the value of the infinite series

annotations.note: Mathlib's canonical definition of the exponential (NormedSpace.exp via NormedSpace.expSeries) is exactly this power series, and NormedSpace.exp_eq_tsum_div/NormedSpace.exp_eq_tsum witness the tsum form.

annotations.label: Inverse of logarithm integral definition

anchor.snippet: Inverse of logarithm integral. Define

annotations.note: Real.log is defined via the exponential order-iso (not as ∫1/t) but Real.exp_log gives the inverse relationship and integral_one_div_of_pos ties the integral to log, so only the definitional route is missing.

annotations.label: Differential equation definition

anchor.snippet: Differential equation. Define

annotations.note: Mathlib proves Real.hasDerivAt_exp (exp'=exp) and Real.exp_zero=1, but does not define exp as the unique solution of y'=y with y(0)=1.

annotations.label: Functional equation definition

anchor.snippet: Functional equation. The exponential function

annotations.note: Real.exp_add / Complex.exp_add formalize the multiplicative property, but exp is not defined by this functional equation plus a regularity+normalization condition.

annotations.label: Elementary definition by powers

anchor.snippet: Elementary definition by powers. Define the exponential function with base

annotations.note: Mathlib's Real.rpow routes through exp/log rather than through a distinguished base b characterized by lim (b^h−1)/h = 1, so this elementary characterization is not present as a definition.

annotations.label: Error of product limit expression

anchor.snippet: The error of the product limit expression is described by

annotations.note: No Mathlib lemma bounds the difference exp(x) − (1+x/n)^n by the leading O(x^2/n) term of this expansion.

annotations.label: Convergence of power series

anchor.snippet: it follows from the ratio test that

annotations.note: NormedSpace.expSeries_radius_eq_top shows the exponential series has infinite radius of convergence, i.e. converges for every argument.

annotations.label: Logarithm integral is a bijection

anchor.snippet: It must be shown that the function from

annotations.note: Real.expOrderIso (exp: ℝ ≃ ℝ>0) and Real.log_injOn_pos witness that log is a bijection ℝ>0 → ℝ, but not via the integral characterization.

annotations.label: Existence of base for elementary definition

anchor.snippet: This limit can be shown to exist for any

annotations.note: The claim that lim (b^h−1)/h exists for every b>0 and yields ln b is not stated in Mathlib as a standalone existence result driving an alternative definition.

annotations.label: Characterization 1 equivalent to Characterization 2

anchor.snippet: The following argument is adapted from Rudin

annotations.note: No Mathlib theorem proves the product-limit form equals the power-series form of exp.

annotations.label: Characterization 1 equivalent to Characterization 3

anchor.snippet: Here, the natural logarithm function is defined in terms of a definite integral

annotations.note: No Mathlib equivalence between the product-limit characterization and the inverse-of-∫1/t characterization is stated.

annotations.label: Characterization 1 equivalent to Characterization 4

anchor.snippet: Applying the simplest form of Euler's method

annotations.note: The Euler-method argument connecting the product-limit definition to the ODE definition is not formalized.

annotations.label: Characterization 2 equivalent to Characterization 4

anchor.snippet: This shows that definition 4 implies definition 2.

annotations.note: hasDerivAt_exp + NormedSpace.exp_zero establish that the power-series exp satisfies the ODE, but the reverse direction (uniqueness giving 4 ⇒ 2) is not packaged as an equivalence.

annotations.label: Characterization 2 implies Characterization 5

anchor.snippet: follows from the term-by-term manipulation of power series justified by uniform convergence

annotations.note: Real.exp_add (and Complex.exp_add / NormedSpace.exp_add_of_commute) prove multiplicativity directly from the power-series definition.

annotations.label: Characterization 3 equivalent to Characterization 4

anchor.snippet: Characterisation 3 first defines the natural logarithm

annotations.note: No Mathlib theorem states the equivalence of the inverse-of-∫1/t definition and the ODE definition of exp.

annotations.label: Characterization 5 implies Characterization 4

anchor.snippet: imply both conditions in characterization 4

annotations.note: No Mathlib result derives the ODE characterization from the multiplicative-plus-regularity characterization.

annotations.label: Characterization 5 implies Characterization 4 (alternate)

anchor.snippet: Assume characterization 5, the multiplicative property together with the initial condition

annotations.note: The alternate proof deducing y'=y from f(x+y)=f(x)f(y) plus f'(0)=1 is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Characterization 5 equivalent to Characterization 6

anchor.snippet: By inductively applying the multiplication rule

annotations.note: Mathlib does not link the multiplicative-property characterization to the b^x elementary characterization as an if-and-only-if.

annotations.label: Lebesgue-integrability implies continuity (5 ⇒ 6)

anchor.snippet: we will prove that Lebesgue-integrability implies continuity

annotations.note: No Mathlib lemma proves that a Lebesgue-integrable solution of the Cauchy multiplicative functional equation is continuous.

annotations.label: Counterexample for complex initial conditions

anchor.snippet: satisfies the three listed regularity conditions in (5) but is not equal to

annotations.note: The counterexample showing that the characterizations fail on ℂ without further conditions is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Natural logarithm as integral

anchor.snippet: is the inverse of the natural logarithm function

annotations.note: Real.log is not defined as ∫1/t in Mathlib, but integral_one_div_of_pos proves ∫ 1/x from a..b = log(b/a) so the integral identity is available.

annotations.label: Extension to complex domain via analytic continuation

anchor.snippet: and then extend it as an analytic function

annotations.note: analyticAt_cexp shows Complex.exp is entire, but Mathlib constructs Complex.exp directly from its series rather than by analytic continuation from Real.exp.

annotations.label: Definitions (1), (2), (4) extend to Banach algebras

anchor.snippet: Definitions (1), (2), and (4) all make sense for arbitrary Banach algebras

annotations.note: NormedSpace.exp and its ODE/series lemmas (hasDerivAt_exp, NormedSpace.exp_eq_tsum) formalize exp on any Banach algebra via the power-series and ODE routes.

annotations.label: Integrand 1/t is integrable, integral well-defined

anchor.snippet: Since the integrand is an integrable function of t , the integral expression is well-defined

annotations.note: Mathlib has integrability of 1/x on intervals avoiding 0 (used implicitly by integral_one_div_of_pos) but no standalone lemma phrased as `1/t is integrable, so the log integral is well-defined`.

annotations.label: Surjectivity via divergence of harmonic series

anchor.snippet: they follow from the integral test and the divergence of the harmonic series

annotations.note: Surjectivity of log ℝ>0 → ℝ is available via Real.expOrderIso, but Mathlib does not derive it from the integral-test/divergence-of-harmonic-series argument.

annotations.label: Intermediate value theorem guarantees existence of base

anchor.snippet: the Intermediate value theorem guarantees the existence of such a value

annotations.note: The Intermediate Value Theorem is formalized as intermediate_value_univ / IsPreconnected.intermediate_value, but its application to produce the exponential base is not.

annotations.label: Binomial theorem bound in Rudin's argument

anchor.snippet: By the binomial theorem

annotations.note: The general binomial theorem (add_pow / Commute.add_pow) is formalized, but its specific use to bound (1+x/n)^n vs the partial sums of exp(x) is not.

annotations.label: Extension of equivalence to negative reals

anchor.snippet: This equivalence can be extended to the negative real numbers

annotations.note: The extension of the product-limit ⇔ power-series equivalence to negative arguments is not formalized.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of calculus applied to ln

anchor.snippet: By the first part of fundamental theorem of calculus

annotations.note: The FTC (intervalIntegral.integral_eq_sub_of_hasDerivAt) and Real.hasDerivAt_log are both formalized, so the specific derivation (d/dx ∫₁^x 1/t = 1/x) is a routine composition, but not packaged as one lemma.

annotations.label: Logarithm power rule ln(a^n) = n ln(a)

anchor.snippet: the result ln a n = n ln a has been used

annotations.note: Real.log_pow states log(x^n) = n * log x exactly.

annotations.label: Euler's method converges to exact solution

anchor.snippet: since Euler's Method is known to converge to the exact solution

annotations.note: Mathlib has ODE_solution_unique but no formalization of the convergence of the Euler discretization to the exact ODE solution.

annotations.label: Nonzero function is nonzero everywhere

anchor.snippet: If [MATH] is nonzero anywhere (say at [MATH] ), then it is non-zero everywhere

annotations.note: No Mathlib lemma proves that a solution of the multiplicative Cauchy equation nonzero at one point is nonzero everywhere.

annotations.label: Continuity at one point implies continuity everywhere

anchor.snippet: If [MATH] is continuous anywhere (say at [MATH] ), then it is continuous everywhere

annotations.note: No Mathlib lemma establishes that a solution of the multiplicative Cauchy equation continuous at one point is continuous everywhere.
